name: curcumin
notes: Natural yellow colour derived from turmeric. Considered safe by EFSA.

name: riboflavin
notes: Natural yellow colour. Also an essential B vitamin. Safe at approved levels.

name: tartrazine
notes: Azo dye linked to hyperactivity in children. Requires warning label in EU: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children'.

name: quinoline yellow
notes: Synthetic dye. Requires hyperactivity warning label in EU. Banned in Australia, USA, Japan.

name: sunset yellow fcf
notes: Azo dye. Requires hyperactivity warning label in EU. May cause allergic reactions, especially in aspirin-sensitive individuals.

name: cochineal
notes: Derived from cochineal insects. Can cause severe allergic reactions including anaphylaxis. Not suitable for vegans.

name: azorubine
notes: Azo dye. Requires hyperactivity warning label in EU. Banned in the USA, Japan, and several other countries.

name: amaranth
notes: Banned in the USA since 1976 due to carcinogen concerns. Restricted in EU to fish roe only. Azo dye.

name: ponceau 4r
notes: Azo dye. Requires hyperactivity warning label in EU. Banned in the USA and Norway.

name: erythrosine
notes: Iodine-containing dye. Restricted in EU to cocktail cherries and candied cherries only. High iodine content may affect thyroid function.

name: allura red ac
notes: Most widely used red azo dye. Requires hyperactivity warning label in EU. EFSA has re-evaluated and determined current ADI needs updating.

name: patent blue v
notes: Synthetic blue dye. Can cause allergic reactions. Banned in the USA and Australia.

name: indigotine
notes: Synthetic indigo dye. Can cause allergic reactions. High doses associated with nausea and hypertension in animal studies.

name: brilliant blue fcf
notes: Synthetic triphenylmethane dye. Generally considered safe at approved levels. Banned in several countries.

name: chlorophylls
notes: Natural green pigment from plants. Considered safe.

name: copper complexes of chlorophyll
notes: Copper-complexed chlorophyll. More stable than natural chlorophyll. Safe at approved levels.

name: plain caramel
notes: Plain caramel produced by heating sugar. Generally recognised as safe.

name: caustic sulphite caramel
notes: Caramel produced with sulphite compounds. May trigger reactions in sulphite-sensitive individuals.

name: ammonia caramel
notes: Produced with ammonia. Contains 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), a potential carcinogen. California Prop 65 listed.

name: sulphite ammonia caramel
notes: Most common caramel colour in colas and dark soft drinks. Contains 4-MEI, a potential carcinogen. California Prop 65 listed.

name: brilliant black bn
notes: Azo dye. Requires hyperactivity warning label in EU. Banned in the USA, Canada, and Japan.

name: vegetable carbon
notes: Vegetable carbon for use in confectionery and cheese rind. Restricted to specific uses in EU.

name: brown ht
notes: Azo dye. Requires hyperactivity warning label in EU. Banned in the USA, Australia, Japan, and several other countries.

name: beta-carotene
notes: Natural orange pigment and provitamin A. Safe at approved levels. Smokers should avoid high-dose supplements.

name: annatto
notes: Natural extract from annatto seeds. EFSA (2020) concluded ADI of 0.1 mg/kg bw/day. Some reports of allergic reactions and IBS aggravation.

name: paprika extract
notes: Natural extract from red peppers. Considered safe by EFSA.

name: lycopene
notes: Natural carotenoid pigment from tomatoes. Considered safe; may have antioxidant benefits.

name: lutein
notes: Natural carotenoid. EFSA ADI 1 mg/kg bw/day. Generally considered safe and potentially beneficial for eye health.

name: beetroot red
notes: Natural red pigment from beetroot. Considered safe.

name: anthocyanins
notes: Natural pigments from fruit and vegetables. Considered safe with potential antioxidant benefits.

name: calcium carbonate
notes: Mineral salt used as colour and anti-caking agent. Also a calcium supplement. Considered safe.

name: titanium dioxide
notes: Banned in EU food use from 2022 (Regulation 2022/63) following EFSA opinion that it cannot be considered safe. Still permitted in some non-EU countries.

name: iron oxides
notes: Mineral pigments restricted to specific foods (e.g. olive paste, salmon). Considered safe at approved levels.

name: silver
notes: Restricted to surface coating of confectionery only. Excessive intake can cause argyria (permanent skin discolouration).

name: gold
notes: Edible gold restricted to surface coating of confectionery and liqueurs. Chemically inert; considered safe.

name: litholrubine bk
notes: Azo dye restricted to surface colouring of cheese rind only in EU.

name: sorbic acid
notes: Naturally occurring preservative. Considered safe at approved levels.

name: potassium sorbate
notes: Salt of sorbic acid. Widely used preservative. Considered safe at approved levels.

name: benzoic acid
notes: Preservative. Can react with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) to form benzene, a carcinogen. Requires hyperactivity warning when combined with certain colours.

name: sodium benzoate
notes: Sodium salt of benzoic acid. Can react with vitamin C to form benzene. Southampton study linked to hyperactivity in children.

name: potassium benzoate
notes: Potassium salt of benzoic acid. Similar concerns to sodium benzoate.

name: calcium benzoate
notes: Calcium salt of benzoic acid. Similar concerns to sodium benzoate.

name: ethyl para-hydroxybenzoate
notes: Paraben preservative. Has weak estrogenic activity. Use in foods is restricted to specific products in EU.

name: sodium ethyl para-hydroxybenzoate
notes: Sodium salt of ethyl paraben. Similar concerns to ethylparaben.

name: methyl para-hydroxybenzoate
notes: Most common paraben preservative. Weak estrogenic activity documented. Restricted in EU cosmetics for certain body area applications.

name: sodium methyl para-hydroxybenzoate
notes: Sodium salt of methylparaben. Similar concerns to methylparaben.

name: sulphur dioxide
notes: Preservative and antioxidant. Major allergen — must be declared on label above 10 mg/kg. Can trigger asthma attacks.

name: sodium sulphite
notes: Sulphite preservative. Declared allergen above 10 mg/kg in food. Can trigger reactions in sensitive individuals.

name: sodium bisulphite
notes: Sulphite preservative. Declared allergen above 10 mg/kg. May trigger asthma in sensitive individuals.

name: sodium metabisulphite
notes: Sulphite preservative widely used in wine, dried fruit. Declared allergen above 10 mg/kg.

name: potassium metabisulphite
notes: Sulphite preservative common in wine and beer making. Declared allergen above 10 mg/kg.

name: calcium sulphite
notes: Sulphite preservative. Declared allergen above 10 mg/kg.

name: calcium bisulphite
notes: Sulphite preservative. Declared allergen above 10 mg/kg.

name: potassium bisulphite
notes: Sulphite preservative. Declared allergen above 10 mg/kg.

name: biphenyl
notes: Fungicide for citrus fruit surface treatment only. Not permitted inside food. IARC Group 3.

name: orthophenyl phenol
notes: Fungicide for citrus fruit surface. Linked to bladder irritation. Restricted to surface treatment of citrus.

name: sodium orthophenyl phenol
notes: Sodium salt of OPP. Fungicide for citrus surface treatment only.

name: nisin
notes: Natural bacteriocin produced by Lactococcus lactis. Safe at approved levels. Restricted to specific products in EU.

name: natamycin
notes: Natural antifungal antibiotic. Permitted on cheese and cured sausage surface only in EU.

name: hexamine
notes: Formaldehyde-releasing preservative. Restricted to marinated herrings and sprats in EU. Banned in the USA.

name: dimethyl dicarbonate
notes: Beverage sterilant. Decomposes rapidly into methanol and CO2 after use. Considered safe when used correctly.

name: potassium nitrite
notes: Used in cured meats. Can form nitrosamines (IARC Group 2A carcinogens) when heated. IARC classifies processed meat as Group 1 carcinogen.

name: sodium nitrite
notes: Primary curing agent in processed meats. Forms nitrosamines. IARC classifies processed meat (Group 1 carcinogen) partly due to nitrites. EFSA has reduced ADI.

name: sodium nitrate
notes: Nitrate preservative. Converts to nitrite in the body. Used in cured meats and cheeses.

name: potassium nitrate
notes: Nitrate preservative in cured meats. Converts to nitrite in the body.

name: acetic acid
notes: Main component of vinegar. Considered safe at food use levels.

name: potassium acetate
notes: Potassium salt of acetic acid. Preservative and acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: sodium acetate
notes: Preservative and acidity regulator. The familiar 'salt and vinegar' flavour. Considered safe.

name: calcium acetate
notes: Calcium salt of acetic acid. Preservative and acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: lactic acid
notes: Naturally occurring acid produced by fermentation. Considered safe.

name: propionic acid
notes: Naturally occurring short-chain fatty acid. Effective mould inhibitor in bread. Considered safe.

name: sodium propionate
notes: Sodium salt of propionic acid. Mould inhibitor in bread and baked goods. Considered safe.

name: calcium propionate
notes: Most common mould inhibitor in bread. Calcium salt of propionic acid. Considered safe.

name: potassium propionate
notes: Potassium salt of propionic acid. Mould inhibitor. Considered safe.

name: boric acid
notes: Restricted to caviar preservation only in EU. Reproductive toxin; classified as SVHC under REACH. High cumulative risk.

name: sodium tetraborate
notes: Restricted to caviar only in EU. Reproductive and developmental toxin. Banned in USA, Australia, and other countries.

name: ascorbic acid
notes: Vitamin C. Antioxidant and nutritional supplement. Considered safe and beneficial.

name: sodium ascorbate
notes: Sodium salt of ascorbic acid. Antioxidant. Considered safe.

name: calcium ascorbate
notes: Calcium salt of ascorbic acid. Antioxidant and calcium supplement. Considered safe.

name: ascorbyl palmitate
notes: Fat-soluble ester of ascorbic acid used as antioxidant in oils and fatty foods. Considered safe.

name: mixed tocopherols
notes: Natural vitamin E mixture. Antioxidant. Considered safe.

name: alpha-tocopherol
notes: Most biologically active form of vitamin E. Antioxidant. Considered safe.

name: gamma-tocopherol
notes: Synthetic gamma tocopherol. Antioxidant and form of vitamin E. Considered safe.

name: delta-tocopherol
notes: Synthetic delta tocopherol. Antioxidant and form of vitamin E. Considered safe.

name: propyl gallate
notes: Antioxidant in fats and oils. Some studies suggest weak estrogenic activity. Can cause allergic reactions.

name: octyl gallate
notes: Antioxidant in fats and oils. Can cause allergic reactions. Some liver effects in animal studies.

name: dodecyl gallate
notes: Antioxidant in fats and oils. Can cause allergic reactions.

name: erythorbic acid
notes: Isomer of ascorbic acid used as antioxidant. Low vitamin C activity. Considered safe.

name: sodium erythorbate
notes: Sodium salt of erythorbic acid. Antioxidant in cured meats. Considered safe.

name: tbhq
notes: Synthetic antioxidant in fats and oils. Some animal studies show carcinogenic effects at high doses. Not approved in Japan.

name: bha
notes: Synthetic antioxidant. IARC Group 2B possible carcinogen. Potential endocrine disrupting properties. California Prop 65 listed.

name: bht
notes: Synthetic antioxidant. Potential carcinogen and endocrine disruptor. Some studies show liver toxicity at high doses.

name: lecithin
notes: Natural emulsifier from soy, eggs, or sunflowers. Considered safe. May be allergen if soy-derived and not labelled.

name: sodium lactate
notes: Sodium salt of lactic acid. Preservative and humectant. Considered safe.

name: potassium lactate
notes: Potassium salt of lactic acid. Preservative and acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: calcium lactate
notes: Calcium salt of lactic acid. Used as firming agent and calcium supplement. Considered safe.

name: citric acid
notes: Naturally occurring acid in citrus fruits. Very widely used acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: sodium citrates
notes: Sodium salts of citric acid. Acidity regulators and emulsifying salts. Considered safe.

name: potassium citrates
notes: Potassium salts of citric acid. Acidity regulators. Considered safe.

name: calcium citrates
notes: Calcium salts of citric acid. Acidity regulators and calcium supplement. Considered safe.

name: tartaric acid
notes: Natural acid found in grapes and wine. Acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: sodium tartrates
notes: Sodium salts of tartaric acid. Acidity regulators. Considered safe.

name: potassium tartrates
notes: Potassium salts of tartaric acid. Cream of tartar is monopotassium tartrate. Considered safe.

name: phosphoric acid
notes: Strong acid used in colas. High phosphate intake linked to bone density loss and cardiovascular risk. Strongly erosive to teeth.

name: sodium phosphates
notes: Sodium phosphate salts. High phosphate intake from additives is associated with kidney and cardiovascular strain.

name: potassium phosphates
notes: Potassium phosphate salts. Similar concerns regarding excess phosphate intake.

name: calcium phosphates
notes: Calcium phosphate salts used as raising agents and calcium supplements. Considered safe.

name: magnesium phosphates
notes: Magnesium phosphate salts. Used as anti-caking agents. Considered safe.

name: sodium malates
notes: Sodium salts of malic acid. Acidity regulators. Considered safe.

name: potassium malate
notes: Potassium salt of malic acid. Acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: calcium malates
notes: Calcium salts of malic acid. Acidity regulators and firming agents. Considered safe.

name: adipic acid
notes: Naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid. Used as acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: succinic acid
notes: Naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid found in amber. Acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: triammonium citrate
notes: Ammonium salt of citric acid. Acidity regulator. Considered safe.

name: calcium disodium edta
notes: Chelating agent. Can bind and deplete essential minerals. Used as preservative in canned foods. Restricted to specific foods.

name: alginic acid
notes: Natural polysaccharide from brown seaweed. Thickener and gelling agent. Considered safe.

name: sodium alginate
notes: Sodium salt of alginic acid. Thickener, gelling agent, and stabiliser. Considered safe.

name: potassium alginate
notes: Potassium salt of alginic acid. Thickener and gelling agent. Considered safe.

name: ammonium alginate
notes: Ammonium salt of alginic acid. Thickener and gelling agent. Considered safe.

name: calcium alginate
notes: Calcium salt of alginic acid. Forms strong heat-stable gels. Considered safe.

name: propylene glycol alginate
notes: Ester of alginic acid with propylene glycol. Thickener and emulsifier. Considered safe.

name: agar
notes: Natural polysaccharide from red algae. Gelling agent. Vegan alternative to gelatin. Considered safe.

name: carrageenan
notes: Seaweed-derived thickener. Degraded form (poligeenan) is a known carcinogen; food-grade carrageenan is debated. Some evidence of gut inflammation and IBD aggravation.

name: locust bean gum
notes: Natural gum from carob seeds. Thickener and gelling agent. Considered safe.

name: guar gum
notes: Natural gum from guar beans. Thickener. Considered safe. Very high doses may cause digestive issues.

name: tragacanth
notes: Natural gum from Astragalus species. Thickener and stabiliser. Considered safe.

name: acacia gum
notes: Natural gum from acacia trees. Emulsifier and thickener. Considered safe. May cause mild digestive effects at high intake.

name: xanthan gum
notes: Bacterial polysaccharide from Xanthomonas campestris. Widely used thickener. Considered safe.

name: karaya gum
notes: Natural gum from Sterculia urens. Thickener and stabiliser. Considered safe.

name: tara gum
notes: Natural gum from tara tree seeds. Thickener. Considered safe by EFSA.

name: gellan gum
notes: Bacterial polysaccharide. Gelling agent and stabiliser. Considered safe.

name: sorbitol
notes: Sugar alcohol. Excessive consumption (>50g/day) has laxative effect. Products with >10% must carry 'excessive consumption may produce laxative effects' warning.

name: mannitol
notes: Sugar alcohol. Laxative warning required. Poorly absorbed; can cause osmotic diarrhoea at high intake.

name: glycerol
notes: Natural polyol found in fats. Humectant and solvent. Considered safe.

name: polysorbate 20
notes: Emulsifier. Studies suggest polysorbates may alter gut microbiota and increase intestinal permeability.

name: polysorbate 80
notes: Widely used emulsifier. Research links polysorbate 80 to gut dysbiosis and increased intestinal permeability in animal studies.

name: polysorbate 40
notes: Emulsifier. Part of the polysorbate family with similar gut microbiome concerns.

name: polysorbate 60
notes: Emulsifier in bakery products and ice cream. Similar concerns to other polysorbates.

name: polysorbate 65
notes: Emulsifier. Part of the polysorbate family.

name: pectin
notes: Natural polysaccharide from fruit cell walls. Gelling agent. Considered safe and beneficial (soluble fibre).

name: ammonium phosphatides
notes: Emulsifier used in chocolate. Derived from ammoniated rapeseed oil phosphatides. Considered safe.

name: diphosphates
notes: Raising agents and emulsifying salts. High dietary phosphate from additives linked to adverse kidney and cardiovascular effects.

name: triphosphates
notes: Emulsifying salt and water retention agent in meat products. High phosphate intake concerns.

name: polyphosphates
notes: Emulsifying salts and stabilisers. Part of the phosphate additive group with concerns about high dietary phosphate.

name: cellulose
notes: Natural plant fibre polymer. Anti-caking agent and bulking agent. Considered safe.

name: methyl cellulose
notes: Modified cellulose. Thickener and gelling agent that gels when heated. Considered safe.

name: hydroxypropyl cellulose
notes: Modified cellulose used as thickener and stabiliser. Considered safe.

name: hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose
notes: Modified cellulose. Thickener, gelling agent, and coating agent. Considered safe.

name: carboxymethyl cellulose
notes: Synthetic cellulose derivative. Animal studies show alterations to gut microbiota. Some concern over IBD promotion at high doses.

name: mono and diglycerides of fatty acids
notes: Emulsifiers derived from glycerol and fatty acids. Widely used. Considered safe.

name: acetic acid esters of mono and diglycerides
notes: Acetic acid ester emulsifiers. Considered safe.

name: lactic acid esters of mono and diglycerides
notes: Lactic acid ester emulsifiers. Considered safe.

name: citric acid esters of mono and diglycerides
notes: Citric acid ester emulsifiers. Considered safe.

name: diacetyl tartaric acid esters
aliases: diacetyl tartaric acid esters of mono and diglycerides
notes: Emulsifier widely used in bread baking. Considered safe.

name: sucrose esters of fatty acids
notes: Emulsifiers derived from sucrose and fatty acids. Considered safe.

name: sucroglycerides
notes: Mixture of sucrose esters and mono/diglycerides. Emulsifier. Considered safe.

name: polyglycerol esters of fatty acids
notes: Emulsifiers derived from polyglycerol and fatty acids. Considered safe.

name: polyglycerol polyricinoleate
notes: Emulsifier derived from castor oil and polyglycerol. Used in chocolate. Considered safe.

name: sodium stearoyl lactylate
notes: Emulsifier used in baked goods and dairy products. Derived from stearic acid and lactic acid. Considered safe.

name: calcium stearoyl lactylate
notes: Calcium salt analogue of SSL. Emulsifier in baked goods. Considered safe.

name: sorbitan monostearate
notes: Emulsifier derived from sorbitol and stearic acid. Considered safe.

name: sorbitan tristearate
notes: Emulsifier used in chocolate coatings. Considered safe.

name: sorbitan monolaurate
notes: Emulsifier. Considered safe.

name: sorbitan monooleate
notes: Emulsifier. Considered safe.

name: sorbitan monopalmitate
notes: Emulsifier. Considered safe.

name: glutamic acid
notes: Naturally occurring amino acid and flavour enhancer. Considered safe. Extremely high intakes may cause transient symptoms in some individuals.

name: monosodium glutamate
notes: Sodium salt of glutamic acid. EFSA (2017) set ADI of 30 mg/kg bw/day. Commonly associated with 'Chinese Restaurant Syndrome' though evidence is mixed. Must be labelled.

name: monopotassium glutamate
notes: Potassium salt of glutamic acid. Flavour enhancer. Similar concerns to MSG.

name: calcium diglutamate
notes: Calcium salt of glutamic acid. Flavour enhancer with similar properties to MSG.

name: monoammonium glutamate
notes: Ammonium salt of glutamic acid. Flavour enhancer.

name: magnesium diglutamate
notes: Magnesium salt of glutamic acid. Flavour enhancer.

name: disodium guanylate
notes: Nucleotide flavour enhancer. Often used with MSG to amplify umami. Contains purines; avoid if you have gout. Derived from fish or yeast.

name: disodium inosinate
notes: Nucleotide flavour enhancer often combined with MSG. Contains purines. Not suitable for gout sufferers.

name: disodium ribonucleotides
notes: Mixture of disodium guanylate and disodium inosinate. Powerful flavour enhancer. Contains purines.

name: acesulfame k
notes: Artificial sweetener. Some animal studies suggest gut microbiome alterations. EFSA considers it safe within ADI (9 mg/kg bw/day).

name: aspartame
notes: Artificial sweetener. IARC (2023) classified as possibly carcinogenic (Group 2B). Must carry PKU warning. Breaks down to phenylalanine, aspartic acid, and methanol.

name: cyclamates
notes: Banned in the USA since 1969 following bladder cancer findings in rats. Banned in EU for table-top use; some EU member states still permit it in certain foods. IARC Group 3.

name: isomalt
notes: Sugar alcohol derived from sucrose. Laxative warning required at high consumption levels. Suitable for diabetics.

name: saccharin
notes: Oldest artificial sweetener. Bladder cancer risk in rats at very high doses not considered relevant to humans. Recent studies suggest gut microbiome disruption.

name: sucralose
notes: Chlorinated sugar. EFSA considers safe within ADI (15 mg/kg bw/day). Some studies link to gut microbiome disruption and altered insulin response.

name: thaumatin
notes: Natural protein sweetener from katemfe fruit. Very intense sweetener. Considered safe.

name: steviol glycosides
notes: Natural sweetener from stevia plant. EFSA ADI 4 mg/kg bw/day. Considered safe.

name: neotame
notes: Synthetic derivative of aspartame. Much more potent. EFSA ADI 2 mg/kg bw/day. Fewer long-term studies than older sweeteners.

name: maltitol
notes: Sugar alcohol with significant glycaemic impact (GI ~35). Laxative effect at high consumption. Warning required.

name: lactitol
notes: Sugar alcohol derived from lactose. Laxative effect at high consumption. Warning required.

name: xylitol
notes: Sugar alcohol. Laxative warning required. Beneficial for dental health. Highly toxic to dogs.

name: erythritol
notes: Sugar alcohol with very low caloric content. 2023 study (Hazen lab) linked high blood erythritol to cardiovascular events. EFSA considers safe but further research is ongoing.
